title: Can Patreon un-enshittify the internet?
gist: Benjamin Clark analyses how Patreon's original creator-monetisation model is being squeezed by Apple/Spotify in-app subscriptions and algorithm-driven feeds that suppress the 'follow' relationship creators relied on. To survive, Patreon is adding discovery features and poaching writers from Substack on a content-moderation differentiator, while AI-generated 'slop' intensifies the engagement-maximising dynamics across platforms.

insights.claim: Patreon's original model — grow a free audience on other platforms, then upsell die-hard fans on exclusive paid content — is now foreclosed to many new entrants as the route to organic audience-building has closed.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: The creator-monetisation funnel is breaking at the top — a structural headwind for subscription-first platforms dependent on external audience growth.
insights.quote: this is now foreclosed to many new entrants

insights.claim: Patreon is now severely threatened because Apple and Spotify offer creators in-app subscription monetisation at lower price points, capturing exclusive content that previously required navigating to Patreon.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Platform owners disintermediating third-party subscription tools is a direct revenue threat — watch Patreon's creator retention vs. Apple/Spotify.
insights.quote: Apple and Spotify now offer creators this option too, and often at a lower price point

insights.claim: Major podcasts such as Goalhanger's The Rest is History and The Rest is Politics have opted for Apple/Spotify in-app monetisation over Patreon, signalling defection of premier creators.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Top-creator defection erodes Patreon's flagship case studies and pricing power.
insights.quote: Some of the world’s biggest podcasts...have opted for such in-app monetisation.
insights.locator: as listeners no longer need to navigate

insights.claim: Patreon was valued at $4bn in its 2021 raise, works with 300,000+ creators, and has paid out over $10bn to date — a meaningful scale in the creator economy.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Baseline scale metrics for valuing Patreon against the in-app monetisation threat.
insights.quote: Patreon was valued at $4bn when it last raised funding in 2021. Now it works with more than 300,000 creators and has paid them more than $10bn in total to date.

insights.claim: Patreon runs no advertising and only earns when creators get paid, taking a 5–12% cut depending on plan, giving it incentives structurally different from ad-funded social platforms.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Aligned-incentive model is Patreon's positioning differentiator vs. Meta/X attention-maximising — a thesis for why it may avoid 'enshittification'.
insights.quote: Patreon doesn’t run any advertising—it only gets paid if users choose to purchase subscriptions from creators (from which Patreon takes a 5 to 12 per cent cut

insights.claim: Substack takes a 10% cut on a similarly aligned model and has the discovery edge — a sizeable reader community plus the X-like Notes feed and recommendation system.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Discovery, not just monetisation rake, is the competitive battleground between Substack and Patreon.
insights.quote: Substack’s additional advantage, however, is its discovery features.

themes.theme: The death of the follow and algorithmic capture

insights.claim: Social feeds have inverted from mostly-followed content to mostly algorithm-suggested content, making it structurally harder for creators to sustain audiences they have already won.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: The decline of the 'follow' relationship is the root cause squeezing all creator-monetisation businesses.
insights.quote: Now the balance has reversed. Our feeds are dominated by what the tech giants think we’ll like, instead of what we’ve told them we want to see.
insights.locator: Consider the quiet death of the “follow” button.

insights.claim: Patreon CEO Jack Conte frames algorithmic platforms as having shifted from 'jukeboxes' to 'slot machines' that A/B test their way to the bottom of the brain stem to maximise ad-viewing time.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Crisp framing of the attention-economy critique that underpins Patreon's marketing positioning.
insights.quote: hacking the human limbic system and A/B testing your way to the bottom of someone’s brain stem and figuring out what will suck them in

insights.claim: Musk's X suppressed posts containing external links (notably to Substack), making it harder for writers to grow audiences off-platform and convert them into subscribers; engagement reportedly remains lower despite a purported backtrack.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Platform link-suppression is a concrete mechanism degrading creator funnel economics across the sector.
insights.quote: He suppressed the visibility of posts with links to external websites (particularly Substack)
insights.locator: Such features have become especially important

insights.claim: Patreon is poaching Substack's top writers using content moderation as a selling point — Conte touts that Patreon disallows the far-right/neo-Nazi content Substack permits with suppressed visibility.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Moderation stance is emerging as a creator-acquisition lever — a differentiated competitive axis to monitor in platform churn.
insights.quote: Patreon doesn’t allow that kind of stuff, which I’m very proud of. That’s been one selling point.
insights.locator: Some writers, for instance, feel uncomfortable

insights.claim: Patreon's strict community guidelines cut both ways — Taylor Lorenz complains they inhibit discussion of controversial topics (porn, violence, politics) that tech journalists need to build audiences.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: The same moderation that attracts some creators repels others — a double-edged positioning risk.
insights.quote: How is any tech journalist supposed to build an audience online when the topics we write about like porn, violence, politics, etc are deemed ‘unsafe’?
insights.locator: Not all of the platform’s user feedback

insights.claim: Patreon secured a partnership with Vox to distribute existing and subscriber-only content, signalling a move toward institutional media deals alongside individual creators.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: Media-company partnerships are a new growth vector beyond solo creators — watch for more institutional deals.
insights.quote: Patreon has also secured a partnership deal with media company Vox

insights.claim: AI is now supercharging algorithms' worst impulses by producing highly engaging but meaningless 'slop' content at unprecedented scale across platforms.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: AI-generated content glut intensifies the attention-capture dynamics that drive creators toward subscription alternatives.
insights.quote: artificial intelligence is now supercharging the worst impulses of the algorithm, presenting highly engaging but ultimately meaningless slop content at an unprecedented scale

insights.claim: Clark predicts a bifurcation: some users sucked deeper into AI-driven feeds while others grow disillusioned and seek subscription-based alternatives — the addressable market thesis for Patreon/Substack.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Clark
insights.so_what: The 'disillusioned-user' segment is the demand-side bet underpinning paid-creator platforms.
insights.quote: others will become increasingly disillusioned and look for alternatives
insights.locator: Some users will be sucked into
